---
title: "11 · Resolution requirements for actionable hazards"
---

The twin runs at 90 m. Is that the resolution these hazards need? The honest answer is that there is
no single number — **landslide, liquefaction, and flood each have a different generation scale, a
different actionable scale, and a different upstream driver that must be resolved before the hazard
model ever sees data.** This page states those scales explicitly and grounds each one in the
literature, then asks what workflow and what twin architecture actually accommodate three different
answers at once.

## Why "actionable" is not "characterized"

Two different questions get conflated:

- **Where is the hazard elevated, region-wide?** — a screening question. Coarse products (90 m–1 km)
  answer this well.
- **Should we evacuate this street, retrofit this bridge, close this specific road?** — an
  actionability question, about one asset or a handful of them. This almost always requires
  finer-than-regional resolution, because the physics that flips a decision (a specific hollow's pore
  pressure, a specific parcel's soil facies, a specific culvert's capacity) lives below what any
  regional product resolves.

Below, **generation scale** is where the physical process that causes the hazard actually operates;
**actionable scale** is what a decision about a specific asset needs; **upstream driver** is the
coarser process that has to be right *before* either of those can be, because errors made there
propagate downstream and cannot be recovered by refining the hazard model alone.

## Landslides

| | scale | why |
|---|---|---|
| **Generation** | 1–10 m, ideally lidar-grade (1–3 m) | Shallow-landslide initiation is controlled by convergent topography — colluvial hollows and first-order channel heads that concentrate subsurface flow and pore pressure [@montgomery1994shalstab]. These features are typically 10–50 m wide; coarsening the DEM measurably degrades shallow-landslide hazard prediction because it smooths exactly this convergent structure away [@claessens2005demresolution]. |
| **Actionable** | parcel/road scale — effectively the DEM resolution above | "This slope above mile marker X" requires resolving that slope, not a coarse average of it. Washington DNR's own landslide-hazard mapping guidance is built on lidar DEMs for this reason [@wadnr2016landslidemapping]. |
| **Upstream driver** | pore pressure / water table at the *same* 1–10 m footprint | A coarse water-table field averages away the local pore-pressure spikes that trigger failure — a regional-scale antecedent-state estimate is a reasonable input to a hillslope-scale hydromechanical model, but not a substitute for one. |

## Liquefaction

| | scale | why |
|---|---|---|
| **Regional screening** | 100 m–1 km | The standard geospatial liquefaction susceptibility models operate at this resolution by design, as rapid-response/regional tools, not site-specific ones [@zhu2015geospatial; @zhu2017geospatial]. |
| **Actionable (site-specific)** | point-scale, via CPT/SPT | Liquefaction triggering depends on facies-level grain-size/density variability (paleochannels, point bars) that no gridded product resolves. Standard semi-empirical triggering procedures are built on point-scale borehole/CPT data for exactly this reason [@idriss2006liquefaction] — this is a site-investigation problem, not one a finer raster can solve. |
| **Upstream driver** | depth to the seasonal-high water table, **vertically**, not horizontally | A few tens of centimetres of error in depth-to-water can flip a cell between "liquefiable" and "not" — the lever that matters is vertical accuracy of the water-table estimate, not horizontal grid spacing. |

## Flooding

| | scale | why |
|---|---|---|
| **Antecedent state / water budget** | 90 m–1 km | How much water is already in the basin, and when, is a volume-and-timing question that coarse hydrologic modelling answers well. |
| **Actionable (local inundation depth)** | 1–5 m, especially in developed areas | Local flood depth — which building floods, by how much — depends on micro-topography (road crowns, culverts, berms) invisible at coarse resolution; DEM resolution and source are shown to materially change inundation-extent accuracy in detailed/urban studies [@cook2009floodmapping]. |
| **Upstream driver** | orographic precipitation enhancement at ridge scale | Precipitation over mountainous terrain like the Cascades is tightly coupled to ridge-scale topography [@roe2003orographic]; a forcing product that has already lost that structure (e.g. a coarse global forecast resampled without orographic physics) cannot regain it downstream, however fine the flood model that consumes it is. |

## The telescope: why upstream resolution matters more than the hazard's own footprint

A model that is fine at the *bottom* of a hazard's causal chain but coarse upstream of it produces a
precise-looking wrong answer, because the error that mattered was made before the fine model ever ran:

```
precipitation forcing (ridge-scale orographic enhancement)
   -> runoff generation (hillslope-scale: convergent topography, variable source area)
      -> channel routing / basin discharge (reach-scale)
         -> local flood depth (1-5 m)  |  landslide triggering (1-10 m)  |  liquefaction screening (100 m-1 km, refined by point CPT)
```

A 1 m flood-inundation model fed by a coarse precipitation forcing that never resolved ridge-scale
orographic enhancement will produce a beautifully detailed *wrong* answer, because the error that
generated the flood was made two steps upstream of where the fine model is looking. The same logic
applies to landslides: a hillslope model run at 3 m resolution is only as good as the coarser
water-table field feeding it pore pressure, and to liquefaction: a facies-resolving site investigation
cannot fix a seasonal-high water table that was wrong by a meter before it ever reached the site.

**The general result:** a single uniform resolution is the wrong target for any of these hazards. Each
needs (a) a coarse, regional product that gets the *volume and timing* of the driving process right,
and (b) a fine, local model that gets the *threshold-crossing* physics right for one asset — and the
fine model is only as good as the coarse one feeding it.

## A workflow that accommodates all three, without three different models

The three hazards above do not need three separate, independently-built systems — they need the same
three-tier structure, instantiated once per hazard:

- **Tier 0 — regional state.** A coarse (90 m–1 km) antecedent-state and susceptibility product,
  updated as observations arrive, covering the whole domain. Answers the screening question: where is
  a hazard class elevated, region-wide, and by how much has recent forcing changed that?
- **Tier 1 — area-scoped fine physics.** A finer (1–10 m) model run only over the specific area of
  interest a decision requires — one watershed, one route corridor, one facility footprint — using
  Tier 0's state as boundary/initial conditions rather than re-deriving it. This is where the
  hazard-specific threshold physics (pore pressure on a hollow, inundation depth at a culvert, LPI at a
  borehole) actually lives.
- **Tier 2 — site data.** Point measurements for one specific asset (a CPT/SPT log, a lidar
  micro-topography survey, a met station) that refine a Tier 1 output further. This tier is inherently
  site-investigation work; no gridded regional product, however fine, replaces it.

Running Tier 1 **only where requested**, rather than everywhere at fine resolution, is what keeps this
computationally manageable: the cost of a fine hazard model scales with the area it covers, and most
real decisions only need one watershed, one corridor, or one footprint at a time, not a fine-resolution
rebuild of the whole regional domain.

## Modularizing a system around this

The architectural consequence is a clean separation of concerns rather than one resolution serving
every purpose:

1. **A Tier 0 producer** that owns the coarse, whole-domain dynamic state and its uncertainty, and
   commits to nothing about how any downstream consumer will use it.
2. **Tier 1 consumers**, each declaring three things once: what Tier 0 fields it needs (and at what
   native resolution and uncertainty, so a downscaled value is never mistaken for an observation);
   what finer resolution and area it operates at (set by its own decision, not by Tier 0's grid); and
   what, if anything, it hands back upstream (a refined hazard probability, an updated confidence mask,
   or nothing at all — a one-way handoff is a legitimate design).
3. **A boundary contract between the two** that always records native-vs-delivered resolution, so
   provenance survives the handoff even when the consumer's grid is much finer than the producer's.

This also explains how the same regional system serves genuinely different users without three
separate products: a regional risk manager wants Tier 0 alone; an engineer responsible for one
structure wants Tier 0 handed to a Tier 2 site investigation; a corridor or network planner wants
Tier 0 handed to a Tier 1 model scoped to their specific route or footprint. The tiering *is* the
modularity — one coarse core, with the resolution mismatch made explicit at each handoff rather than
hidden inside a single "one true resolution" grid that tries, and fails, to be simultaneously right for
a regional screen and a site-specific decision.
